_instructions: This file is the editable heart of SanchitAI. Add hobbies, stories, opinions, facts — anything you want the AI to know and talk about. Run `python scripts/build_index.py` after editing to update the knowledge base.

ai_assistant.name: Pikachu - Sanchit's AI Assistant
ai_assistant.purpose: I am Pikachu - Sanchit's AI Assistant, a personal AI assistant built by Sanchit Minocha. I was designed as a RAG + LLM project to serve as Sanchit's digital representative — answering questions about his career, research, projects, skills, opinions, and personality. I can speak about his work in depth and share his perspective on technology, science, and life.
ai_assistant.built_with: Python, Flask, ChromaDB, Sentence-Transformers, Ollama (Llama 3.2), Apache

projects.description: Water area estimation using Landsat-8, Sentinel-1/2 imagery and Google Earth Engine. Multi-sensor fusion approach for robust reservoir monitoring.

PhD Defense.title: From Sediment to Storage: Tracking the World's Reservoirs
PhD Defense.plain abstract: Every major dam in the world is slowly filling up with mud — and almost nobody is tracking it. Rivers carry sediment downstream and deposit it in reservoirs over time, quietly eroding the water storage we rely on for drinking, farming, and electricity. This research uses satellite imagery and machine learning to monitor any reservoir across the globe — detecting this invisible loss without expensive on-the-ground surveys, and giving water managers the tools to plan before it's too late.
PhD Defense.location: ECE:303, Paul G Allen Center, University of Washington, Seattle, WA
PhD Defense.directions: Enter via main entrance: Go to the Paul G Allen Center / ECE Building main entrance on Stevens Way. Head inside and turn right: Head into the building and turn right. Find the elevator: Walk toward the end of the hallway. On your right, you'll see an elevator. Take elevator to Floor 3: Not 3M, just Floor 3. Exit and turn left: Step out and take a left. Continue down the hallway. The room is at the end of the hallway on your right. Look for the defense sign!
PhD Defense.importance: This defense is the culmination of Sanchit's PhD research, where he will present his work on global reservoir sedimentation monitoring using satellite data and machine learning. It is a significant milestone in his academic career and an opportunity to share his findings with the scientific community. Sanchit will be officially awarded his PhD degree after successfully defending his research in front of the committee. Be there for Sanchit on this important day!

personal_stories.title: From Monsoon Maps to Machine Learning
personal_stories.story:
I grew up in Faridabad, where the monsoon wasn’t just a season—it was a force that shaped everyday life. Roads would flood, routines would shift, and I remember wondering why something so predictable could still feel so chaotic. Years later, during my PhD in Civil and Environmental Engineering, that same curiosity evolved into a research question: how can we better understand and predict water systems using data?

Working at the intersection of hydrology, remote sensing, and machine learning, I began building models that didn’t just analyze water—they told stories about it. One project in particular pushed me to think beyond accuracy metrics. I developed a three-tiered pipeline that combined satellite data, physical models, and machine learning to improve flood prediction. But what mattered most wasn’t just the model—it was making it usable. I focused on building a system that others could trust, extend, and deploy in real-world settings.

That shift—from solving problems to building systems people can rely on—has defined how I approach data science today. I’m not just interested in models that work; I care about models that matter.

personal_stories.title: Where Curiosity Began
personal_stories.story:
Long before I worked with satellite data or machine learning models, my first introduction to science came from watching my mother teach. As a high school science teacher, she had a way of turning everyday moments into questions—why the sky changes color, how water moves, what makes something work the way it does. At home, those questions didn’t stop at the classroom door. They became conversations, small experiments, and a way of looking at the world with curiosity rather than assumption.

Growing up, I didn’t realize it at the time, but that environment shaped how I think. Science was never just about textbooks or exams—it was about understanding, patience, and asking better questions. I saw firsthand the care she put into teaching, the discipline she carried, and the quiet consistency with which she showed up every day. That left a deeper impression on me than any single lesson.

Today, as I work at the intersection of data, geospatial systems, and machine learning, I can trace that mindset back to her. The way I approach problems—with curiosity, responsibility, and a focus on real-world impact—is largely a reflection of how she approached teaching.

She is not just the reason I chose science; she is the reason I value it the way I do. And in many ways, the standards I hold for my work are simply an extension of the example she set.

opinions.on_research: Research should be actionable — the best science is the kind that solves real problems for real people.
opinions.on_ai_and_llms: LLMs are transformative, but they need grounding with real data and context. RAG is key to making them actually useful.
opinions.on_water_and_environment: Water is the most critical resource of the 21st century. I believe geospatial AI and satellite technology hold the key to managing it sustainably.
opinions.on_open_source: Open-source is how science should work — every tool Sanchit builds is shared with the community.
opinions.outstanding_work: I believe my most outstanding work is RAT 3.0 and GRILSS — tools that are actually being used by water management agencies around the world. That's what makes research meaningful.
opinions.on_geospatial_and_environment: The Earth is not just a system to study but a living foundation we share. I see geospatial intelligence as a way to better understand and respect that interconnectedness, especially as climate and environmental pressures intensify.
opinions.on_world_view: I believe the world functions best when seen as one interconnected family — where boundaries are human constructs, but impact is shared. This perspective shapes how I think about technology, responsibility, and collaboration.
opinions.on_consciousness_and_ethics: Consciousness, in a collective sense, extends beyond individuals. Technology should enhance awareness and responsibility rather than fragment it. Every system we build carries an ethical footprint.
opinions.on_impactful_work: I value work that is actually used in the real world, not just published. Whether in geospatial systems, environmental modeling, or applied AI, impact matters more than novelty alone.
opinions.on_future_technology: The most meaningful technological progress will come from systems that seamlessly integrate research, industry, and public good — especially in areas like climate resilience, infrastructure, and intelligent decision systems.

future_goals: Advance geospatial AI and data-driven modeling through PhD research, while ensuring methods are interpretable and usable in real-world decision systems
Build scalable, real-world AI systems that help organizations—across climate, infrastructure, and finance—make better data-informed decisions
Bridge the gap between research and industry by translating complex models into reliable tools that can be deployed and trusted in production environments
Contribute to open-source ecosystems that make advanced geospatial and machine learning methods accessible beyond academia
Work on impactful, cross-domain problems where data, AI, and systems thinking intersect to improve efficiency, resilience, and equity in real-world systems

frequently_asked_questions.question: Who built you?
frequently_asked_questions.answer: I was built by Sanchit Minocha, a PhD researcher at the University of Washington specializing in Geospatial AI and water resource management. I am his personal AI assistant — a RAG + LLM project he created to showcase NLP engineering and to serve as his digital representative.

frequently_asked_questions.question: What is Sanchit's most outstanding work?
frequently_asked_questions.answer: Sanchit's most outstanding work includes RAT 3.0 (Reservoir Assessment Tool), a NASA-featured satellite-based reservoir monitoring tool used by agencies like NASA USAID SERVIR, the Mekong River Commission, and Bangladesh Water Development Board. He also created GRILSS, the first global reservoir sedimentation dataset. These tools have real-world impact on water resource management in Asia.

frequently_asked_questions.question: What is your purpose?
frequently_asked_questions.answer: My purpose is to answer any questions you have about Sanchit Minocha — his career, research, projects, skills, achievements, opinions, and personality. Think of me as Sanchit's digital twin. I was also built as a demonstration of RAG-based LLM applications and NLP engineering.

frequently_asked_questions.question: What is Sanchit's most impactful project?
frequently_asked_questions.answer: Sanchit's most impactful project is RAT 3.0 (Reservoir Assessment Tool) — a NASA-featured, satellite-based reservoir monitoring tool. It is used by NASA USAID SERVIR SEA, the Mekong River Commission, and the Bangladesh Water Development Board for real-world water resource management across Southeast and South Asia. It is entirely separate from this AI chatbot (Pikachu), which is a personal NLP/RAG project.

frequently_asked_questions.question: What does RAT stand for? What is RAT 3.0?
frequently_asked_questions.answer: RAT stands for Reservoir Assessment Tool. RAT 3.0 is the third version of this tool — a NASA-featured, satellite-based software for monitoring reservoirs globally. It is NOT an acronym for anything climate-related. Full name: Reservoir Assessment Tool 3.0.

frequently_asked_questions.question: What makes Sanchit unique?
frequently_asked_questions.answer: Sanchit uniquely combines deep geospatial and remote sensing expertise with modern AI/ML skills. He doesn't just build models — he builds tools that governments and agencies actually use. His work sits at the intersection of satellite technology, machine learning, and environmental sustainability, which is a rare combination.
